RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 6, 1996

Cristina L. Talley
Acting City Attorney
City of Pasadena
100 North Garfield Avenue
Post Office Box 7115
Pasadena, California  91109-7215

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-96-123

Dear Ms. Talley:

     This is in response to your request on behalf of Pasadena City Councilmembers William Crowfoot, Chris Holden and Paul Little and Planning Commissioners Harden Carter, Bill Fay and Margaret Thomas for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

     1.  Is the vote on the North Lake Specific Plan a "decision" under the Act?

     2.  To what extent may members of the City Council and the Planning Commission, who live in proximity to the proposed boundaries of the Specific Plan, participate in decisions relating to the Plan?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The votes on the Specific Plan are "decisions" under the Act.

     2.  Each aspect of the Specific Plan decisions must be analyzed to determine whether the decision will have a material financial effect on each official's interests.  The officials must

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18995.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-123
Page 2

abstain from participating in any decision that will have a
material financial effect on their economic interest.

FACTS²

1. The Specific Plan

The City of Pasadena is presently considering the draft North
Lake Specific Plan for adoption. The draft North Lake Specific
Plan Area is an area in the center of the City of Pasadena along a
major north-south street. It is approximately 1.5 miles in length
and approximately .5 miles at its widest point. It is located
almost entirely in one city council district, but portions of it
are within three other city council districts.

The draft North Lake Specific Plan is a legislative planning
tool used to accomplish the systematic implementation of the
general plan for a primarily commercial corridor in the City of
Pasadena. The draft North Lake Specific Plan does not change the
features or nature of the underlying property as would a
redevelopment plan; it does not create any special fund dedicated
to the improvement of the Plan area as would a redevelopment plan;
rather, it discusses and proposes land uses, inventories public
and private facilities and programs, and sets forth all
coordinates ongoing programs and implementation measures. The
self-identified goals of the draft North Lake Specific Plan are as
follows:

By providing for good community design, particular
emphasis is given to the establishment of design
criteria in harmony with community cohesion and
neighborhood identity. Identification of areas for
mixed-use development and mitigation of traffic impacts
are some of the goals the Specific Plan addresses.

Other goals of the Specific Plan are:

° To move away from auto-oriented uses
° To create a pedestrian-friendly environment
° To develop streetscape and landscape plans
° To develop design standards
° To protect residential areas from impacts of

_________________

2 These facts are taken from a draft letter you sent us outlining
your analysis of the conflict of interest issues involved. In
addition, Nicholas Rodriguez of your office sent me a copy of the
Specific Plan. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Accordingly, we used the Specific Plan only to illustrate or
confirm facts contained in your letter. We did not undertake an
independent review of the Specific Plan to find additional
potential conflict problems.

File No. A-96-123
Page 3


                    commercial development
                    ° To create a safe environment.

     In addition to the broad policy goals in the Plan, there are
several specific proposals.

     a.  Rezoning to Office Commercial.  Approximately 33 parcels
on Lake Avenue between Elizabeth Street and Ladera Street and
approximately 10 parcels on Washington Boulevard are currently
Limited Commercial and will be rezoned to Office Commercial.

     b.  Service Station Abatement.  Currently, the above-
described Limited Commercial parcels intended for rezoning to
Office Commercial contain automobile service stations.  Pursuant
to the plan, the service stations will be abated "within a period
long enough for amortization commensurate with the investment
involved in the properties." (Specific Plan, p. E-2.)

     c.  Rezoning to Limited Commercial.  Approximately 56 parcels
of land on Lake Avenue between an area just north of Orange Grove
Boulevard and Maple Street will be rezoned from General Commercial
to Limited Commercial.  In addition, property at the corner of
Lake Avenue and Villa Street will be rezoned from multi-family
residential to Limited Commercial.

     d.  Multi-Family Residential Rezoning.  Approximately 12
parcels of land on Villa Street will be rezoned from Multi-family
Residential 32 units per acre to Multi-family residential 48 units
per acre.

     e.  Tree Improvements.  The Specific Plan also contains
extensive plans for installation of trees along Lake Avenue.  The
tree installation is described in several stages.  By the time the
last stage is complete in 1999, tree lanes and peninsulas will
have been installed along the entire length of Lake Avenue
contained within the Specific Plan.

     f.  Overlay Zone.  The Specific Plan also modifies the lists
of permitted and conditionally permitted uses with a Specific Plan
overlay.  The Plan sets out a new list of permitted and
conditionally permitted uses in the Office Commercial and Limited
Commercial zones within the Specific Plan.

     g.  Inventory of Activity.  The Plan also inventories all of
the activity going on within the Plan boundaries.  Already in
place are a number of ongoing city improvements to and activity
within the Plan area, including already planned signal
improvements, already planned street resurfacing, past
installation of bicycle racks, continued restructuring of a
shopping center, facade improvement, continued assistance to a
shared parking lot, development of a four unit housing project, a
number of studies, continued staffing at a neighborhood park and
similar activities.  The North Lake Specific Plan is not the
source of these activities; rather, it is a tool for identifying

File No. A-96-123
Page 4


the manner in which these ongoing activities should be seen by the
public as complementary and embodying continued public commitment
to the area.

All of the mentioned activity was approved through the
separate legislative and administrative channels for the
identification of priorities and allocation of public resources.
The North Lake Specific Plan does not supplant or shortcut these
channels and plays no role in the allocation of these resources.

2.  Officials Involved

All of the interested individuals are city councilmembers or
planning commissioners of the City of Pasadena, a city with
135,240 residents.  The city council has seven members, all of
whom are elected by district.  Each district has approximately
19,000 residents.  Planning commissioners are appointed by the
city council either as representatives or by the nomination of the
Mayor.  The North Lake Avenue Specific Plan is almost entirely
within City Council District Number 5, but parts of it rest in
Districts 1, 2 and 3.

APPLICABLE LAW

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in, or using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.  (Section 87100.)  A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on, among other
things:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

     (b)  Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1000) or more.

     (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public

File No. A-96-123
Page 5


official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a)-(d).

An official's residence is an interest in real property, as
defined in Section 82033 as follows:

"Interest in real property" includes any
leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property
located in the jurisdiction owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official,
or other filer, or his or her immediate family if
the fair market value of the interest is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more ....

1. Making, Participating in Making or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

The city councilmembers and planning commissioners make a
governmental decision whenever they vote on a matter, commit the
city to a course of action, or enter into any contractual
agreement on behalf of the city. (Regulation 18700 (b).) In
addition, an official participates in a governmental decision
when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the
official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.

Regulation 18700(c)

Finally, under Regulation 18700.1(a), an official uses his or
her official position to influence a decision if:

File No. A-96-123
Page 6


     [F]or the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise
attempts to influence, any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf
of a business entity, client, or customer.

     If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a
material financial effect on the official's home and property
interest, the official must disqualify himself or herself from
making or participating in making that decision. The official
also may not use his or her official position to influence the
decision.

2.  Foreseeability

     The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required; however, an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community
Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)

3.  Materiality

     The Commission has adopted several regulations which define
material financial effect. Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for determining whether an official's financial
interest in a decision is "material" as required by Section 87103.
If the official's financial interest is directly involved in the
decision, Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality. If
the official's financial interest is indirectly affected by the
decision, Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 apply to determine
whether the effect of the decision is material.

     The effect of a decision on real property or a business
entity in which an official has a direct, indirect or beneficial
ownership interest, is material if the official's property is
directly involved in a decision. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).)

     Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) provides a list of situations in
which an official's real property is directly involved in a
decision. Some examples include decisions to zone or rezone the
official's property, decisions to annex the property, decisions
concerning assessments on the property and decisions to include
the property in or exclude it from any city, county or other local
governmental decision.

     The effect of a decision on a business entity is material if
the business entity initiates the proceeding, is a named party in
the proceeding or if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of a permit, license or contract.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)

File No. A-96-123
Page 7


Regulation 18702.3 contains guidelines for determining when
the effect of a decision is material as to real property which is
indirectly involved in the decision. Regulation 18702.3 states in
pertinent part:

(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the fol-
lowing applies:

(1) The real property in which the of-
ficial has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction
of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.

(3) The real property in which the of-
ficial has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or

(B) Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.

Under Regulation 18702.3(d), there are a number of factors
which must be considered in determining whether the decision will
have a financial effect of $10,000 or more. These factors
include, but are not limited to:

(1) The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of

File No. A-96-123
Page 8


the proposed project or change in use in relationship to the property in which the official has an
interest;

(2) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;

(3) In addition to the foregoing, in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, effects on traffic, view,
privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.


Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance for determining whether
a nonprofit entity will be materially financially affected by a
governmental decision. An official who has an investment in, or
receives income from, a nonprofit corporation must consult the
financial thresholds in Regulation 18702.5 to determine whether a
decision will have a material financial effect on the nonprofit.

4.    Public Generally

Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. For the "public generally"
exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's
interests in substantially the same manner as it would affect a
significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.)

Pursuant to Regulation 18703(a), the material financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisions (a)(1) and (a)(2) below apply:

(1) Significant Segment: The governmental
decision will affect a "significant segment" of the
public generally as set forth below:

(A) The decision will affect:

(i) Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(ii) Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the

File No. A-96-123
Page 9


official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

(iii) Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents, so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,

(B) The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,

(C) The decision will affect the
segment of persons identified in
California Code of Regulations, Title 2,
Section 18703.2; or,

(D) The decision will affect a
segment of the population which does not
meet any of the standards in subdivisions
(a)(1)(A) through (a)(1)(C), however, due
to exceptional circumstances regarding
the decision, it is determined such
segment constitutes a significant segment
of the public generally.

(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The
governmental decision will affect the
official's economic interest in substantially
the same manner as it will affect the economic
interests of the segment identified in
subdivision (a)(1) of this regulation.

Thus, for the public generally exception to apply, each
official must show that the decision will affect a significant
segment of the public in their jurisdiction and that the decision
will affect the official's economic interest in substantially the
same manner as the segment of the public.

ANALYSIS

1. Decisions

Generally, each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that
there will be a material financial effect on an official's
economic interests. (DH-1 Owen (1976) 2 PPC Ops. 77.) The
official must carefully analyze each decision to determine the
effect on the official's economic interests.

You argue in your draft opinion that the Specific Plan is not
a "decision" under the Act. Regulation 18700(b) provides that a

File No. A-96-123
Page 10


public official makes a decision when he or she votes on any
matter or commits his or her agency to a course of action. The
Commission has consistently advised that adoption of a specific
plan is a decision under the Act. Advice Letter, No. A-94-
204; Crotch Advice Letter, No. A-93-135; and Woodhead Advice
Letter, No. A-90-768.) Moreover, the California Supreme Court has
stated that the adoption of a specific plan is a legislative act,
merely an administrative act. (Yost v. Thomas (1984) 36
Cal.3d 561.) It is indisputable that the officials will be making
a "decision" when they vote on the Specific Plan.

If an official plans to vote on the Specific Plan as a whole
in one vote and that official has a conflict as to any one of the
decisions contained within the Plan, then the official is
disqualified.

2.  Economic Interests

a.  Planning Commissioner Fay. Planning Commissioner Fay
represents District One on the Planning Commission. Commissioner
Fay has a real property interest, his residence, within 300 feet
of the boundary of the Specific Plan. Commissioner Fay lives
within 300 feet of tree improvements planned for Lake Avenue
between Howard Street and Washington Boulevard and within 300 feet
of property slated for rezoning between Elizabeth Street and
Ladera Street.

In addition, Commissioner Fay lives within 2,500 feet of
property slated for rezoning from Limited Commercial to Office
Commercial. Commissioner Fay lives within 2,500 feet of current
auto repair shops slated for abatement as well as within 2,500
feet of additional tree improvements on Lake Avenue.

b.  Planning Commissioner Carter. Planning Commissioner
Carter is the "at-large" representative on the Planning
Commission. He lives within 300 feet of the border of the
Specific Plan area. He lives within 2,500 feet of the property
slated for rezoning between Elizabeth and Ladera Streets. He also
lives within 2,500 feet of the property slated for rezoning at
Lake and Washington, as well as the auto repair industry use
abatement.

c.  Councilmember Crowfoot. Councilmember Crowfoot lives
within 2,500 feet from the border of the Specific Plan. He lives
within 2,500 feet of the property slated for rezoning at Lake and
Elizabeth and Washington and Lake, as well as the auto repair
industry abatement in the same vicinity. He lives within 2,500
feet of the tree improvements on Lake. Your map indicates that
Councilmember Crowfoot lives just a few blocks from tree
improvements planned between Washington and Mountain, as well as
an area slated for elimination of automobile repair and gasoline
stations. In addition, you mentioned that Councilmember Crowfoot
lives within 2,500 feet of 39 parcels which are being rezoned from
Limited Commercial to Office Commercial.

File No. A-96-123
Page 11


d.  Councilmember Holden.  Councilmember Holden has a real
property interest in his residence within 2,500 feet of the
Specific Plan area.  He lives within 2,500 feet of the parcels
slated for rezoning at Orange Grove and Lake, extending south to
Maple.  He is also within 2,500 feet of tree improvements on Lake.

     Your facts and the map indicate that Councilmember Holden
lives a few blocks from the planned tree improvements at Lake and
Maple.  In addition, you note that Councilmember Holden lives
within 2,500 feet from 69 parcels which will be rezoned from
General Commercial to Limited Commercial, from Multi-Family
Residential to Limited Commercial or from Multi-Family Residential
32 Units to Multi-Family Residential 48 Units.

e.  Planning Commissioner Thomas.  Commissioner Thomas has a
real property interest in her residence within 2,500 feet of the
Specific Plan area.  Her residence is within 2,500 feet of the
parcels slated for rezoning at Lake and Washington, as well as the
auto repair industry abatement planned for the same area.  She is
also within 2,500 feet of the parcels slated for rezoning at Lake
and Elizabeth Streets and the auto repair industry abatement.  She
is within 2,500 feet of tree improvements on Lake Avenue.

f.  Councilmember Little.  Councilmember Little has two
economic interests which may be affected by the Specific Plan.
First, he has a residential real property interest within 2,500
feet of the plan boundaries.  Councilmember Little lives within a
few blocks of the automobile services abatement area and within
300 feet of the tree improvements slated for Lake Avenue between
Claremont and Washington.  In addition, you indicated he lives
within 2,500 feet of 39 parcels proposed for rezoning from Limited
Commercial to Office Commercial.

     Second, Councilmember Little's source of income is a
nonprofit corporation located within 2,500 of the Specific Plan.
Your facts indicate that the museum is located 2,500 feet from any
planned rezoning and is separated from the Specific Plan Area by a
freeway and a major east-west boulevard.

3.  Materiality

     None of the officials have an economic interest within the
boundaries of the Specific Plan itself.  In addition, none of the
officials' property or source of income is directly involved as
defined in Regulation 18702.1(a) or (b).  Since none of the
officials' economic interests are directly involved in the
decisions, you must apply Regulation 18702.3 to determine whether
their interests will be materially financially affected by any of
the decisions in the plan.

     Because the Specific Plan contains several decisions, the
officials must consider each decision on its own to determine
whether the official has a conflict with respect to the particular

File No. A-96-123
Page 12


decision.  (Yorde Advice Letter, No. A-93-486.)  To determine
whether any decision in the Specific Plan will have such an effect
on his or her real property interest, the officials must consider
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result
in a change in the character of the neighborhood by affecting such
things as the traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions or similar traits.  (Regulation 18702.3(d).)

     In your draft opinion, you find no materiality because the
"the proposed zoning is not designed to result in a change to the
character of the neighborhood, but, rather, to, 'exclude certain
uses which [impair] the relationship between the Specific Plan
area and neighboring residential areas.'"  (Specific Plan, pp. E-3,
E-4.)  The regulation requires the public official to consider
issues such as intensity of use, air emissions or noise levels in
determining materiality.  Even if the Plan is not intended to
alter the neighborhood, if the decisions change the intensity of
use, air emissions, noise levels or any other traits of the
official's residential neighborhood which affects the fair market
value of an official's property by $10,000 or the property's
rental value by $1,000 per month, the official must disqualify.
If the official's interest is within 300 feet, the official may
not participate unless the decision will have no financial effect
on the official's property.

     Planning Commissioners Pay and Carter own real property
within 300 feet of the boundary of the project area.  Since it is
foreseeable that decisions within 300 feet of her property will
have some financial effect on their property, the effect of the
decision will be considered material.

     If the official's interest is within 2,500 feet and outside
300 feet, the official may not participate if the decision will
affect the value of the official's property by $10,000 or more, or
the rental value of the property by $1,000 in a 12-month period.

     Planning Commissioner Thomas and Councilmembers Crowfoot,
Holden and Little own real property that is more than 300 feet but
within 2,500 feet of decisions in the Specific Plan area.  Thus,
the decisions will be material only if the decision will affect
the value of the official's property by $10,000 or more, or the
rental value of the property by $1,000 in a 12 month period.³


_____________________

³    Commissioners Pay and Carter have property within 300 feet of
the Specific Plan and are thus disqualified unless they can show
the decision will have no material financial effect on their
property.  Therefore, you do not need to apply the 2,500 foot
materiality tests to these officials.  If, however, you segment
the decisions as discussed below, you will need to apply
Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) to any decisions more than 300 feet but
less than 2,500 feet from the officials' real property interests.

File No. A-96-123
Page 13


     Finally, you must apply the financial thresholds in
Regulation 18702.5 to determine whether any decisions in the
Specific Plan will have a material financial effect on
Councilmember Little's source of income, a nonprofit corporation.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that any decision has a material
financial effect on the corporation, Councilmember Little must
disqualify himself from that decision.

     4.  Public Generally

     You have not provided us with sufficient facts to analyze the
effect on the public generally. In your draft opinion, you
provide some population data, but the information is not
sufficient to apply the public generally regulation. You
determine that public generally applies to all of the decisions
and all of the officials based on aggregate amounts of the
population affected, a method of calculating public generally that
is not contained within the regulation. For instance, you
repeatedly calculate the number of residents affected in the city
and then divide that number equally by the number of districts
even nominally affected by the Specific Plan. Regulation 18703
permits the official to calculate the number of property owners,
home owners or households either by the entire jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents. The
official must choose one of these methods.⁴

     You also determine that the auto repair abatement activity
affects the public generally because the number of individuals
affected "exceeds the number of 5,000 individuals which the
regulations define as a significant number, per se . . .
[a]lthough not all of those residents are property owners,
are property owners which are not residents . . ." (Draft Letter,
page 13.) This is a misreading of the regulation. Regulation
18703(a) provides that a decision affects a significant segment if
the Medicaid will affect 5,000 individuals who are residents of
the jurisdiction." (Regulation 18703(a)(1)(B), emphasis added.)
Thus, in calculating the 5,000 individuals, you may not consider
property owners who are not residents, such as owners of
commercial property who do not live in the area affected. If you
determine any of the decisions will affect 5,000 residents in the
same manner as the public officials affected, then the public
generally exception will apply.


___________________________

4    This is particularly important in this case. The Specific Plan
area is primarily located in one district, District Five. Parts
of the plan impact Districts One, Two and Three, but to a lesser
extent. Thus, the decisions in the plan will necessarily impact
more property owners or residents in District Five than in
Districts One, Two or Three.

File No. A-96-123
Page 14


5.  Segmentation of Decisions

     There are two ways a city body may vote on a Specific Plan.
The city may consider each of the decisions in separate votes if
the decisions are appropriate for segmentation or the city may
vote on the entire plan in one vote.  If an official determines
that he or she has a conflict in any one of the decisions in the
specific plan and if the city votes on the entire plan at once,
then the official may not participate in any consideration of the
Specific Plan.  If the city is able to segment the decisions,
however, an official may participate in individual decisions in
which he or she does not have a conflict.

     We have, for example, previously advised that large and
complex decisions may, under certain circumstances, be divided
into separate decisions⁵ so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still
participate as to other components in which the official has no
financial interest.  (Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-542;
Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)  Thus, for example, if a
decision concerned parking and pedestrian access ways within 2,500
feet from the nearest boundary of the office building, and this
decision was not interrelated to decisions concerning other,
closer portions of the specific plan, the official could
participate if the decisions would not affect the value of the
building by $10,000 or more or will affect the rental value by
$1,000 or more in a 12-month period.

     Where decisions concerning the Specific Plan may be
considered separately, the following procedure should be followed
to permit the councilmember's participation:

     a.  The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from the other
decisions;

     b.  The decisions for which the councilmember is disqualified
must be considered first, and a final decision reached without his
or her participation;

     c.  Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
Specific Plan in which the councilmember has a disqualifying
interest, he or she may participate in the deliberations regarding

     5  Under some circumstances, a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be segmented.  (Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-
119.)  For example, if a decision concerning one portion of the
specific plan could decide or alter a decision for which the
councilmember has a conflict of interest, the councilmember would
be disqualified as to both decisions.  (Nord Advice Letter,
No. A-82-038; Scher Advice Letter, No. A-88-479.)  Further, where
two decisions are alternatives, the decisions are too interlinked
to be considered separately.

File No. A-96-123
Page 15


other portions of the specific plan, so long as those
deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect
the decisions from which she was disqualified. (Huffaker Advice
Letter, supra.)6

6. Implementation Decisions

In addition, some decisions may be merely implementation
decisions which may be separable from substantive decisions in the
project. (Wachob Advice Letter, No. I-91-464.)

In decisions pertaining to a project, the Commission has
advised that once the "basic policy decisions" in a plan or
manner have been reached, the official "may participate in the
decisions which implement, but do not change these policies."
(Athan Advice Letter, No. A-86-094, copy enclosed.) In the
Portello Advice Letter, No. A-92-471 (copy enclosed) we advised
that an official could participate in a decision regarding the
financing of a theater complex to the extent that the decision did
not affect the existence of the project as a whole and would not
independently materially affect an economic interest of the
official.

You stated in your draft opinion that the Specific Plan
inventories a number of ongoing city improvements and activity
within the plan area. All of this activity, according to your
letter, was previously approved. If this is the case, then the
mere listing of these improvements in the Specific Plan will be
considered an implementation decision which does not affect the
existence of these projects. The officials need not disqualify
themselves from participating in the decision to list these
projects in the Specific Plan so long as the inventory would not
independently materially affect an economic interest of the
officials.


6 However, to the extent that decisions involve the adoption of a
Specific Plan, we have advised that a public official would not be
able to vote on the final adoption of the plan where he or she is
disqualified from participating in specific aspects of the plan
since those aspects of the plan may materially affect his or her
real property interests in unusual ways. (Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)(D); Lanzone Advice Letter, No. A-93-135.)

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell

sections.facts:
2
1.
The Specific Plan
The City of Pasadena is presently considering the draft North
Lake Specific Plan for adoption.
The draft North Lake Specific
Plan Area is an area in the center of the City of Pasadena along a
major north-south street.
It is approximately 1.5 miles in length
and approximately
.5 miles at its widest point.
It is located
almost entirely in one city council district, but portions of it
are within three other city council districts.
The draft North Lake Specific Plan is a legislative planning
tool used to accomplish the systematic implementation of the
general plan for a primarily commercial corridor in the City of
Pasadena.
The draft North Lake Specific Plan does not change the
features or nature of the underlying property as would a
redevelopment plan; it does not create any special fund dedicated
to the improvement of the Plan area as would a redevelopment plan;
rather, it discusses and proposes land uses,
inventories public
and private facilities and programs,
and sets forth and
coordinates ongoing programs and implementation measures.
The
self-identified goals of the draft North Lake Specific Plan are as
follows:
By providing for good community design, particular
emphasis is given to the establishment of design
criteria in harmony with community cohesion and
neighborhood identity.
Identification of areas for
mixed-use development and mitigation of traffic impacts
are some of the goals the Specific Plan addresses.
Other goals of the Specific Plan are:
o
To move away from autà—öriented uses
O
To create a pedestrian—friendly environment
o
To develop streetscape and landscape plans
o
To develop design standards
O
To protect residential areas from impacts of
2
These facts are taken from a draft letter you sent us outlining
your analysis of the conflict of interest issues involved.
In
addition, Nicholas Rodriguez of your office sent me a copy of the
Specific Plan.
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice.
(In re Oglesbv
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
Accordingly,
we used the Specific Plan only to illustrate or
confirm facts contained in your letter.
We did not undertake an
independent review of the Specific Plan to find additional
potential conflict problems.


File No.
A—96—123
commercial development
0
To create a safe environment.
In addition to the broad policy goals in the Plan, there are
several specific proposals.
a.
Rezoning to Office Commercial.
Approximately 33 parcels
on Lake Avenue between Elizabeth Street and Ladera Street and
approximately 10 parcels on Washington Boulevard are currently
Limited Commercial and will be rezoned to Office Commercial.
b.
Service Station Abatement.
Currently,
the above-
described Limited Commercial parcels intended for rezoning to
Office Commercial contain automobile service stations.
Pursuant
to the plan,
the service stations will be abated “within a period
long enough for amortization commensurate with the investment
involved in the properties.”
(Specific Plan,
p.
E-2.)
c.
Rezoning to Limited Commercial.
Approximately 56 parcels
of land an Lake Avenue between an area just north of Orange Grove
Boulevard and Maple Street will be rezoned from General Commercial
to Limited Commercial.
In addition, property at the corner of
Lake Avenue and Villa Street will be rezoned from multi-family
residential to Limited Commercial.
d.
Multi-Family Residential Rezoning.
Approximately 12
parcels of land on Villa Street will be rezoned from Multi-family
Residential
32 units per acre to Multi-family residential 48 units
per acre.
e.
Tree Improvements.
The Specific Plan also contains
extensive plans for installation of trees along Lake Avenue.
The
tree installation is described in several stages.
By the time the
last stage is complete in 1999, tree lanes and peninsulas will
have been installed along the entire length of Lake Avenue
contained within the Specific Plan.
f.
Overlay Zone.
The Spècif Ic Plan also modifies the lists
of permitted and conditionally permitted uses with a Specific Plan
overlay.
The Plan sets out a new list of permitted and
conditionally permitted uses in the Office Commercial and Limited
Commercial zones within the Specific Plan.
g.
Inventory of Activity.
The Plan also inventories all of
the activity going on within the Plan boundaries.
Already in
place are a number of ongoing city improvements to and activity
within the Plan area,
including already planned signal
improvements,
already planned street resurfacing, past
installation of bicycle racks,
continued restructuring of a
shopping center,
facade improvement,
continued assistance to a
shared parking lot,
development of a four unit housing project,
a
number of studies,
continued staffing at a neighborhood park and
similar activities.
The North Lake Specific Plan is not the
source of these activities; rather, it is a tool for identifying


File No.
A—96—123
the manner in which these ongoing activities should be seen by the
public as complementary and embodying continued public commitment
to the area.
All of the mentioned activity was approved through the
separate legislative and administrative channels for the
identification of priorities and allocation of public resources.
The North Lake Specific Plan does not supplant or shortcut these
channels and plays no role in the allocation of these resources.
.2.
Officials Involved
All of the interested individuals are city councilmembers or
planning commissioners of the City of Pasadena,
a city with
135,240 residents.
The city council has seven members, all of
whom are elected by district.
Each district has approximately
19,000 residents.
Planning commissioners are appointed by the
city council either as representatives or by the nomination of the
Mayor.
The North Lake Avenue Specific Plan is almost entirely
within City Council District Number 5,
but parts of it rest in
Districts 1,
2 and 3.
APPLICABLE
LAW
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat
ing in,
or using his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his immediate family or on,
among other
things:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dol
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received
by or promised to the public official within
12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public


File No.
A—96—123
official is a director, officer, partner,. trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
-
Section 87103(a)—(d)
An official’s residence is an interest in real property,
as
defined in Section 82033 as follows:
“Interest in real property” includes any
leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property
located in the jurisdiction owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official,
or other filer,
or his or her immediate family if
the fair market value of the interest is one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more
1.
Making, Participating in Making or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision
The city councilmembers and planning commissioners make a
governmental decision whenever they vote on a matter,
commit the
city to a course of action,
or enter into any contractual
agreement on behalf of the city.
(Regulation 18700
(b).)
In
addition,
an official participates in a governmental decision
when,
acting within the authority of his or her position, the
official:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant substan—
tive review, with a governmental entity or private
person regarding the decision;
or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any
investigation which tequires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision;
or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise of judgment on the
part of the official or designated employee
and the purpose of which is to influence the
decision.
Regulation 18700 (c)
Finally,
under Regulation 18700.1(a),
an official uses his or
her official position to influence a decision if:


File No.
A—96—123
[Fjor the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts,
or appears before,
or otherwise
attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency.
Attempts to
influence include,
but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf
of a business entity, client,
or customer.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a
material financial effect on the official’s home and property
interest
the official must disqualify himself or herself from
making or participating in making that decision.
The official
also may not use his or her official position to influence the
decision.
V
V
2.
V
Forseeability
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required; however,
an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downèy Community
Development Com.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
3.
Materiality
The Commission has adopted several regulations which define
material financial effect.
Regulation 18702 sets forth the
general guidelines for determining whether an official’s financial
interest in a decision is “material
11 as required by Section 87103.
If the official’s financial interest is directly involved in the
decision,
Regulation 18702.1 applies to determine materiality.
If
the official’s financial, interest is indirectly affected by the
decision,
Regulations 18702.2 through 18702.6 apply to determine
whether the effect of the decision is material.
The effect of a decision on real property or a business
entity in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial
ownership interest,
is material if the official’s property is
directly involved in a decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3).)
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3)
provides a list of situations in
which an official’s real property is directly involved in a
decision.
Some examples include decisions to zone or rezone the
official’s property,
decisions to annex the property, decisions
concerning assessments on the property and decisions to include
the property in or exclude it from any city,
county or other local
governmental decision.
V
The effect of a decision on a business entity is material if
the business entity initiates the proceeding, is a named party in
the proceeding or if the decision involves the issuance,
renewal,
approval,, denial or revocation of a permit,
license or contract.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)


File No.
A—96—123
Regulation 18702.3 contains guidelines for determining when
the effect of a decision is material as to real property which is
indirectly involved in the decision.
Regulation 18702.3 states in
pertinent part:
(a)
The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest
(not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the fol
lowing applies:
(1)
The real property in which the of
ficial has an interest,
or any part of that
real property,
is located within a
300 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official’s real property interest.
(2)
The decision involves construction
of,
or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities,
and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.
(3)
The real property in which the of
ficial has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or
(B)
.Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per
12
month period.
Under Regulation 18702.3(d), there are a number of factors
which must be considered in determining whether the decision will
have a financial effect of $10,000 or more.
These factors
include,
but are not limited to:
(1).
The proximity of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the magnitude of


File No.
A—96—123
the proposed project or change in use in relation—
ship to the property in which the official has an
interest;
(2)
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will affect the development potential
or income producing potential of the property;
(3)
In addition to the foregoing,
in the case
of residential property, whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will result in a
change to the character of the neighborhood includ
ing,
but not limited to, effects on traffic,
view,
privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emis
sions,
or similar traits of the neighborhood.
Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance for determining whether
a nonprofit entity will be materially financially affected by a
governmental decision.
An official who has an investment in,
or
receives income from,
a nonprofit corporation must consult the
financial thresholds in Regulation 18702.5 to determine whether a
decision will have a material financial effect on the nonprofit.
4.
Public Generally
Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
For the “public generally”
exception to apply,
a decision must affect the official’s
interests in substantially the same manner as it would affect a
significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.)
Pursuant to Regulation 18703(a),
the material financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official’s economic
interests is indistinguishable from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisioñs (a) (1)
and
(a) (2)
below apply:
(1)
significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
•
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the


File No.
A—96—l23
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade,
or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
(C)
The decision will affect the
segment of persons identified in
California Code of Regulations, Title 2,
Section 18703.2; or,
(D)
The decision will affect a
segment of the population which does not
meet any of the standards in subdivisions
(a) (1) (A)
through
(a) (1) (C),
however,
due
to exceptional circumstances regarding
the decision, it is determined such
segment constitutes a significant segment
of the public generally.
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the
official’s economic interest in substantially
the same manner as it will affect the economic
interests of the segment identified in
subdivision
(a) (1)
of this regulation.
Thus,
for the public generally exception to apply,
each
official must show that the decision will affect a significant
segment of the public in theirjurisdiction
that the decision
will affect the official’s economic interest in substantially the
same manner as the segment of the public.
sections.analysis:
1.
Decisions
Generally,
each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that
there will be a material financial effect on an official’s
economic interests.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77.)
The
official must carefully analyze each decision to determine the
effect on the official’s economic interests.
You argue in your draft opinion that the Specific Plan is not
a “decision” under the Act.
Regulation 18700(b)
provides that a


File No.
A—96—123
public official makes
a decision when he or she votes on any
matter or commits his or her agency to a course of action.
The
Commission has consistently advised that adoption of a specific
plan is
a decision under the Act.
(Kuhn Advice Letter,
No.
A-94-
204;
Crocker Advice Letter,
No.
A—93—l35;
and Woodhead Advice
Letter,
No.
A—90-768.)
Moreover,
the California Supreme Court has
stated that the adoption of a specific plan is a legislative act,
not merely an administrative act.
(Yost v.
Thomas
(1984)
36
Cal.3d 561.)
It is indisputable that the officials will be making
a “decision” when they vote on the Specific Plan.
If an official plans to vote on the Specific Plan as a whole
in one vote and that official has a conflidt as to any one of the
decisions contained within the Plan,
then the official is
disqualified.
2.
Economic Interests
a.
Planning Commissioner Fay.
Planning Commissioner Fay
represents District One on the Planning Commission.
Commissioner
Fay has
a real property interest, his residence, within 300 feet
of the boundary of the Specific Plan.
Commissioner Fay lives
within
300 feet of tree
improvements planned for Lake Avenue
between Howard Street and Washington Boulevard and within
300 feet
of properties slated for rezoning between Elizabeth Street and
Ladera Street.
In addition,
Commissioner Fay lives within 2,500 feet of
property slated for rezoning from Limited Commercial to Office
Commercial.
Commissioner Fay lives within 2,500 feet of current
auto repair shops slated for abatement as well as within 2,500
feet of additional tree improvements on Lake Avenue.
b.
Planning Commissioner Carter.
Planning Commissioner
Carter is the “at—large” representative on the Planning
Commission.
He lives within 300 feet of the border of the
Specific Plan area.
He lives within 2,500 feet of the property
slated for rezoning between Elizabeth and Ladera Streets.
He also
lives within 2,500 feet of the property slated for rezoning at
Lake and Washington,
as well as the auto repair industry use
abatement.
c.
Councilmember Crowfoot.
Councilmember Crowfoot lives
within 2,500 feet from the border of the Specific Plan.
He lives
within 2,500 feet of the property slated for rezoning at Lake and
Elizabeth and Washington and Lake,
as well as the auto repair
industry abatement in the same vicinity.
He lives within 2,500
feet of the tree improvements on Lake.
Your map indicates that
Councilmember Crowfoot lives just a few blocks from tree
improvements planned between Washington and Mountain,
as well as
an area slated for elimination of automobile repair and gasoline
stations.
In addition,
you mentioned that Councilmember Crowfoot
lives within 2,500 feet of
39 parcels which are being rezoned from
Limited Commercial to Office Commercial.


File No.
A—96—123
d.
Councilinember Holden.
Councilmeinber Holden has a real
property interest in his residence within 2,500 feet of the
Specific Plan area.
He lives within 2,500 feet of the parcels
slated for rezoning at Orange Grove and Lake,
extending south to
Maple.
He is also within 2,500 feet of tree improvements on Lake.
Your facts and the map indicate that Councilmember Holden
lives a few blocks from the planned tree improvements at Lake and
Maple.
In addition,
you note that Councilmeinber Holden lives
within 2,500 feet from 69 parcels which will be rezoned from
General Commercial to Limited Commercial,
from Multi-Family
Residential to Limited Commercial or from Multi-Family Residential
32 Units to Multi-Family Residential 48 Units.
e.
Planning Commissioner Thomas.
Commissioner Thomas has a
real property interest in her residence within 2,500 feet of the
Specific Plan area.
Her residence is within 2,500 feet of the
parcels slated for rezoning at Lake and Washington,
as well as the
auto repair industry abatement planned for the same area.
She is
also within 2,500 feet of the parcels slated for rezoning at Lake
and Elizabeth Streets and the auto repair industry abatement.
She
is within 2,500 feet of tree improvements on Lake Avenue.
f.
Councilmember Little.
Coundilmember Little has two
economic interests which may be affected by the Specific Plan.
First,
he has a residential real property interest within 2,500
feet of the Plan boundaries.
Councilmeinber Little lives withina
few blocks of the automobile services abatement area and within
300 feet of the tree improvements slated for Lake Avenue between
Claremont and Washington.
In addition,
you indicated he lives
within 2,500 feet of
39 parcels proposed for rezoning from Limited
Commercial to Office Commercial.
Second,
Councilmember’ Little’s source of income is a
nonprofit corporation located within 2,500 of the Specific Plan.
Your facts indicate that the museum is located 2,500 feet from any
planned rezoning and is separated from the Specific Plan Area by a
freeway and a major east—west boulevard.
3.
Materiality
None of the officials have an economic interest within the
boundaries of the Specific Plan itself.
In addition,
none of the
officials’ property or source of income is directly involved as
defined in Regulation 18702.1(a)
or
(b).
Since none of the
officials’
economic interests are directly involved in the
decisions,
you must apply Regulation 18702.3 to determine whether
their interests will be materially financially affected by any of
the decisions in the plan.
Because the Specific Plan contains several decisions, the
officials must consider each decision on its own to determine
whether the official has a conflict with respect to the particular


File No.
A—96—123
decision.
(Yorde Advice Letter,
No. A-93-486..)
To determine
whether any decision in th Specific Plan will have such an effect
on his or her real property interest, the officials must consider
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result
in a change in the character of the neighborhood by affecting such
things as the traffic, view, privacy,
intensity of use,
noise
levels, air emissions or similar traits.
(Regulation 18702.3(d).)
In your draft opinion,
you find no materiality because the
“the proposed zoning is not designed to result in a change to the
character of the neighborhood, but, rather, to,
‘exclude certain
uses which
[impair] the relationship between the Specific Plan
area and neighboring residential areas.”
(Specific Plan,
pp.
E-3,
E-4.)
The regulation requires the public official to consider
issues such as intensity of use, air emissions or noise levels in
determining materiality.
Even if the Plan is not intended to
alter the neighborhood, if the decisions change the intensity of
use, air emissions, noise levels or any other traits of the
official’s residential neighborhood which affect the fair market
value of an official’s property by $10,000 or the property’s
rental value by $1,000 per month, the official must disqualify.
If the official’s interest is within 300 feet, the official may
not participate unless the decision will have no financial effect
on the official’s property.
Planning Commissioners Fay and Carter own real property
within 300 feet of the boundary of the project area.
Since it is
foreseeable that decisions within 300 feet of her property will
have some financial effect on their property, the effect of the
decision will be considered material.
If the official’s interest is within 2,500 feet and outside
300 feet, the official may not participate if the decision will
affect the value of the official’s property by $10,000 or more,
or
the rental value of the property by $1,000 in a 12-month period.
Planning Commissioner Thomas and Councilmembers Crowfoot,
Holden and Little own real property that is more than 300 feet but
within 2,500 feet of decisions in the Specific Plan area.
Thus,
the decisions will be material only if the decision will affect
the value of the official’s property by $10,000 or more,
or the
rental value of the property by $1,000 in a
12 month period.
3
Commissioners Fay and Carter have property within 300 feet of
the Specific Plan and are thus disqualified unless they can show
the decision will have no material financial effect on their
property.
Therefore,
you do not need to apply the 2,500 foot
materiality tests to these officials.
If,
however,
you segment
the decisions as discussed below,
you will need to apply
Regulation 18702.3(a) (3)
to any decisions more than 300 feet but
less than 2,500 feet from the officials’ real property interests.


File No.
A—96—123
Finally,
you must apply the financial thresholds in
Regulation 18702.5 to determine whether any decisions in the
Specific Plan will have a material financial effect on
Councilmember Little’s source of income,
a nonprofit corporation.
If it is reasonably foreseeable that any decision has a material
financial effect on the corporation,
Councilmember Little must
disqualify himself from that decision.
4.
Public Generally
You have not provided us with sufficient facts to analyze the
effect on the public generally.
In your draft opinion,
you
provide some population data,
but the information is not
sufficient to apply the public generally regulation.
You
determine that public generally applies to all of the decisions
and all of the officials based on aggregate amounts of the
population affected,
a method of calculating public generally that
is not contained within the regulation.
For instance,
you
repeatedly calculate the number of residents affected in the city
and then divide that number equally by the number of districts
even nominally affected by the Specific Plan.
Regulation 18703
permits the official to calculate the number of property owners,
home owners or households either by the entire jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the official represents.
The
official must choose one of these methods.
4
You also determine that the auto repair abatement activity
affects the public generally because the number of individuals
affected “exceeds the number of 5,000 individuals whibh the
regulations define as a significant number, per se
.
[a]lthough not all of those residents are property owners, there
are property owners which are not residents
.
. .“
(Draft Letter,.)
This is a misreading of the regulation.
Regulation
18703(a)
provides that a decision affects a significant segment if
the “decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are residents of
the jurisdiction.”
(Regulation 18703 (a) (1) (B),
emphasis added.)
Thus,
in calculating the 5,000 individuals,
you may not consider
property owners who are not residents,
such as owners of
commercial property who do not live in the area affected.
If you
determine any of the decisions will affect 5,000 residents in the
same manner as the public officials affected,
then the public
generally exception will apply.
This is particularly important in this case.
The Specific Plan
area is primarily located in one district, District Five.
Parts
of the plan impact Districts One,
Two and Three,
but to a lesser
extent.
Thus, the decisions in the plan will necessarily impact
more property owners or residents in District Five than in
Districts One,
Two or Three.


File No.
A—96—123
5.
Seqinentation of Decisions
There are two ways a city body may vote on a Specific Plan.
The city may consider each of the decisions in separate votes if
the decisions are appropriate for segmentation or the city may
vote on the entire plan in one vote.
If an official determines
that he or she has a conflict in any one of the decisions in the
Specific Plan and if the city votes on the entire plan at once,
then the official may not participate in any consideration of the
Specific Plan.
If the city is able to segment the decisions,
however,
an official may participate in individual decisions in
which he or she does not have a conflict.
We have,
for example, previously advised that large and
complex decisions may,
under certain circumstances,
be divided
into separate decisions
5 so that an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still
participate as to other components in which the official has no
financial interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter,
No.
1-90—542;
Huffaker Advice Letter,
No.
A-86-343.)
Thus,
for example,
if
a
decision concerned parking and pedestrian access ways within 2,500
feet from the nearest boundary of the office building,
and this
decision was not interrelated to decisions concerning other,
closer portions of the Specific Plan,
the official could
participate if the decisions would not affect the value of the
building by $10,000 or more or will affect the rental value by
$1,000 or more in a 12-month period.
Where decisions concerning the Specific Plan may be
considered separately, the following procedure should be followed
to permit the councilmember’s participation:
a.
The decisions for which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest must be segregated from the other
decisions;
b.
The decisions for which the councilmember is disqualified
must be considered first,
and a final decision reached without his
or her participation;
c.
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
Specific Plan in which the councilmember has a disqualifying
interest,
he or she may participate in the deliberations regarding
Under some circumstances,
a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be segmented.
(Miller Advice Letter,
No.
A—82-
119.)
For example, if a decision concerning one portion of the
Specific Plan could decide or alter a decision for which the
councilmember has a conflict of interest, the councilmember would
be disqualified as to both decisions.
(Nord Advice Letter,
No.
A-82-038;
Scher Advice Letter,
No.
A-88-479.)
Further,
where
two decisions are alternatives, the decisions are too interlinked
to be considered separately.


File No.
A—96—l23
other portions of the specific plan,
so long as those
deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect
the decisions from which she was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice
Letter, supra.)
6
6.
Implementation Decisions
In addition,
some decisions may be merely implementation
decisions which may be separable from substantive decisions in the
project.
(Wachob Advice Letter,
No.
1-91-464.)
In decisions pertaining to a project, the Commission has
advised that once the “basic policy decisions” to build or
maintain have been reached, the official
“may participate in the
decisions which implement, but do not change these policies.”
(Athan Advice Letter,
No.
A-86-094,
copy enclosed.)
In the
Martello Advice Letter,
No.
A-92-47l
(copy enclosed)
we advised
that an official could participate in a decision regarding the
financing of a theater complex to the extent that the decision did
not affect the existence of the project as a whole and would not
independently materially affect an economic interest of the
official.
You stated in your draft opinion that the Specific Plan
inventories a number of ongoing city improvements and activity
within the Plan area.
All of this activity,
according to your
letter,was previously approved.
If this is the case, then the
mere listing of these improvements in the Specific Plan will be
considered an implementation decision which does not affect the
existence of these projects.
The officials need not disqualify
themselves from participating in the decision to list these
projects in the Specific Plan so long as the inventory would not
independently materially affect an economic interest of the
officials.
6
However,
to the extent that decisions involve the adoption of a
Specific Plan,
we have advised that a public official would not be
able to •vote on the final adoption of the plan where he or she is
disqualified from participating in specific aspects of the plan
since those aspects of the plan may materially affect his or her
real property interests in unusual ways.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (3) (D);
Lanzone Advice Letter,
No.
A—93-135.)


File No.
A—96—123

QUESTION: 1.
Is the vote on the North Lake Specific Plan a “decision”
under the Act?
2.
To what extent may members of the City Council and the
Planning Commission,
who live in proximity to the proposed
boundaries of the Specific Plan, participate in decisions relating
to the Plan?

CONCLUSION: 1.
The votes on the Specific Plan are “decisions” under the
Act.
2.
Each aspect of the Specific Plan decisions must be
analyzed to determine whether the decision will have a material
financial effect on each official’s interests.

embedding.first_500_words: RAVI MEHTA CHAIRMAN FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION May 6, 1996 Cristina L. Talley Acting City Attorney City of Pasadena 100 North Garfield Avenue Post Office Box 7115 Pasadena, California 91109-7215 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-96-123 Dear Ms. Talley: This is in response to your request on behalf of Pasadena City Councilmembers William Crowfoot, Chris Holden and Paul Little and Planning Commissioners Harden Carter, Bill Fay and Margaret Thomas for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Is the vote on the North Lake Specific Plan a "decision" under the Act? 2. To what extent may members of the City Council and the Planning Commission, who live in proximity to the proposed boundaries of the Specific Plan, participate in decisions relating to the Plan? CONCLUSIONS 1. The votes on the Specific Plan are "decisions" under the Act. 2. Each aspect of the Specific Plan decisions must be analyzed to determine whether the decision will have a material financial effect on each official's interests. The officials must _______________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000-18995. 428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 File No. A-96-123 Page 2 abstain from participating in any decision that will have a material financial effect on their economic interest. FACTS² 1. The Specific Plan The City of Pasadena is presently considering the draft North Lake Specific Plan for adoption. The draft North Lake Specific Plan Area is an area in the center of the City of Pasadena along a major north-south street. It is approximately 1.5 miles in length and approximately .5 miles at its widest point. It is located almost entirely in one city council district, but portions of it are within three other city council districts. The draft North Lake Specific Plan is a legislative planning tool used to accomplish the systematic implementation of the general plan for a primarily commercial corridor in the City of Pasadena. The draft North Lake Specific Plan does not change the features or nature of the underlying property as would a redevelopment plan; it does not create any special fund dedicated to the improvement of the Plan area as would a redevelopment plan; rather, it discusses and proposes land uses, inventories public and private facilities and programs, and sets forth all coordinates ongoing programs and implementation measures. The self-identified goals of the draft North Lake Specific Plan are as follows: By providing for good community design, particular emphasis is given to the establishment of design criteria in harmony with community cohesion and neighborhood identity. Identification of areas for mixed-use development and mitigation of traffic impacts are some of the goals the Specific Plan addresses. Other goals of the Specific Plan are: ° To move away from auto-oriented uses ° To create a pedestrian-friendly environment ° To develop streetscape and landscape plans ° To develop design
